text: The video displays an ICG fluorescent angiogram of an ilioanal J pouch in a patient with ulcerative colitis. The J pouch is visible in situ, with two loops of ileum stapled side-to-side, and the apex of the pouch joined to the anorectal stump.
The patient had previously had a total colectomy and now presented for proctectomy with the restoration of ileoanal pouch as their definitive procedure.

Indocyanine Green is injected, revealing intense fluorescence uptake in the ileal aspect of the J pouch. The upper left image shows the standard white light view, the middle left displays the near-infrared fluorescence view, and the main screen and lower left present a real-time augmented reality view with superimposed near-infrared over white light. The images demonstrate excellent perfusion of the ileal pouch, indicating no tension on the pouch with the anastomosis in situ.

Further information on the use of near infrared laparoscopy and endoscopy in contemporary practice can be found in a special supplement of colorectal disease and other videos on this topic are also viewable free of charge on this YouTube channel.
The laparoscope pans around the abdomen, revealing fluorescence in the retroperitoneum and liver due to ICG excretion into the bile. The liver fluorescence is expected to persist for several hours.

The endoscopic view shows the internal aspects of the ileoanal pouch using a near-infrared laparoscope placed via a sigmoidoscope. The mucosal surface of the pouch exhibits strong fluorescence, particularly visible along the staple line of the side-to-side ileoileal anastomosis and the anoileal circular anastomosis.
Patient did have a defunctioning loop ileostomy but convalesced excellently following the procedure and now had the ileostomy closed with satisfactory pouch function.
